In 1906 HMS Dreadnought made every other battleship on Earth obsolete the day she launched, forcing entire navies to start over.
USS William D Porter accidentally fired a live torpedo at the battleship carrying President Roosevelt in 1943. Iowa turned hard and dodged it.
Operation Mincemeat fooled Hitler with a corpse carrying fake invasion plans, floated ashore in Spain by a British submarine in 1943.
America's Ghost Army deceived the Germans with inflatable tanks, sound trucks, and fake radio traffic, and stayed classified for decades.
Navajo code talkers sent secure battlefield messages in their own language across the Pacific war. Japan never broke the code.
At Midway in 1942, American dive bombers fatally wrecked three Japanese aircraft carriers in the space of about five minutes.
Bismarck, Germany's most powerful battleship, was doomed by a slow canvas-covered biplane whose torpedo jammed her rudder.
Wojtek the bear was formally enlisted in the Polish army and carried artillery shells during the battle of Monte Cassino.
Britain's decoding of the Zimmermann Telegram, a German offer of American territory to Mexico, helped pull the United States into World War One.
Polish mathematicians cracked the German Enigma cipher in 1932 and handed their work to Britain and France just before the war began.
Nelson's body came home from Trafalgar preserved in a cask of brandy so the fallen admiral could receive a state funeral.
The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 is the shortest war on record. It lasted roughly forty minutes before Zanzibar surrendered.
Japanese officer Hiroo Onoda kept fighting World War Two in the Philippine jungle until 1974, when his former commander flew out to relieve him.
Finnish sniper Simo Hayha was credited with over 500 kills in under 100 days of the Winter War, mostly using plain iron sights.
Audie Murphy, among the most decorated American soldiers of World War Two, later played himself in the Hollywood film of his exploits.
Leyte Gulf in 1944 was the largest naval battle of modern times and included the last occasion battleships ever fired on each other.
Off Samar in 1944, a handful of American destroyers and escort carriers charged a Japanese battleship fleet head-on and turned it away.
Yamato, the heaviest battleship ever built, carried 18-inch guns yet was sunk by carrier aircraft without ever dueling an enemy battleship.
USS Constitution earned the name Old Ironsides when British cannonballs seemed to bounce off her hull. She is still a commissioned warship.
Confederate submarine Hunley became the first sub to sink an enemy warship in 1864, then vanished with her whole crew that same night.
German submarine U-1206 was lost in 1945 after a malfunctioning high-tech toilet forced her to surface in view of Allied aircraft.
During the Cuban Missile Crisis, Soviet officer Vasili Arkhipov refused to approve firing a nuclear torpedo from a submarine under attack.
In 1983 Soviet officer Stanislav Petrov judged a satellite warning of incoming American missiles to be a false alarm. Sunlight had fooled the sensors.
Operation Paul Bunyan sent a convoy of troops, helicopters, and bombers to protect engineers cutting down one poplar tree in the Korean DMZ.
Australia sent soldiers with machine guns against emus in 1932. The birds scattered, soaked up thousands of rounds, and effectively won.
France's Maginot Line worked exactly as designed in 1940. The German army simply drove around it through the Ardennes forest.
Double agent Juan Pujol Garcia invented a fake spy network so convincing that he received an Iron Cross from Germany and an MBE from Britain.
Mad Jack Churchill went to war with a longbow, bagpipes, and a broadsword, insisting an officer without a sword was improperly dressed.
At Arnhem in 1944, British major Digby Tatham-Warter led attacks carrying an umbrella, joking that it proved he was English.
Days after Hitler died, American and German soldiers fought side by side at Castle Itter, defending French prisoners from the SS.
On Christmas 1914, British and German troops left their trenches to swap gifts, sing carols, and in places kick a football around no man's land.
Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor missed every American aircraft carrier. All were at sea, and they went on to decide the Pacific war.
Russia's Baltic Fleet sailed 18,000 miles around the world in 1905, only to be annihilated by Japan at Tsushima within a day of arriving.
On its way to fight Japan, Russia's Baltic Fleet panicked in the North Sea and shelled British fishing boats, nearly starting a second war.
HMS Campbeltown rammed the Nazi dry dock at Saint Nazaire with explosives hidden in her bow. She blew up a day later with Germans aboard.
America spent about two million dollars developing bat bombs. Escaped armed bats burned down part of the test airfield.
Psychologist B F Skinner trained pigeons to guide missiles by pecking at a target image. The system worked in tests but was never deployed.
Soviet forces trained dogs to carry explosives under German tanks, a program that backfired because the dogs had trained around Soviet vehicles.
The US Army ran a Camel Corps in the 1850s, importing camels to Texas for desert supply routes. The Civil War ended the experiment.
Liberty ship Robert E Peary went from keel to launch in four days and fifteen hours in 1942, and she genuinely sailed and served.
Eisenhower called Andrew Higgins, designer of the flat-bottomed landing craft, the man who won the war for the Allies.
Red Ball Express convoys trucked fuel around the clock during the Allied breakout across France. Most of the drivers were Black American soldiers.
For D-Day the Allies towed two prefabricated harbors across the Channel, then laid a fuel pipeline under the sea to follow the armies.
The Siege of Leningrad lasted 872 days. Scientists there starved to death protecting a priceless seed bank they refused to eat.
Statistician Abraham Wald urged armoring bombers where returning planes had no bullet holes, since planes hit in those spots never came back.
Bomber All American flew home and landed after a mid-air collision left its tail section nearly severed from the fuselage.
Charles Lightoller survived the Titanic as an officer, then decades later sailed his own yacht to Dunkirk and brought home over 120 soldiers.
Planners of the Dunkirk evacuation hoped to rescue 45,000 men. Nine days of small boats and destroyers brought out more than 338,000.
HMS Hood exploded three minutes into her duel with Bismarck. Of the 1,418 men aboard, three survived.
USS Samuel B Roberts, lost charging battleships at Samar, lies nearly 7,000 meters down, the deepest shipwreck ever located.
Cruiser USS Indianapolis secretly delivered components of the first atomic bomb, then was torpedoed and sunk only days later.
Roland Garros, namesake of the tennis stadium, was a fighter pilot and the first to shoot a machine gun through a spinning propeller.
Soviet Night Witches flew wooden biplanes on night raids, cutting their engines to glide silently over German lines before releasing bombs.
Lyudmila Pavlichenko, a Soviet sniper credited with 309 kills, toured the United States in 1942 and befriended Eleanor Roosevelt.
Chuck Yeager broke the sound barrier while hiding two broken ribs, using a sawn-off broom handle to shut the cockpit hatch.
B-52 bombers entered service in 1955 and are slated to fly into the 2050s, on track to become century-old combat aircraft.
The standard evasive move for an SR-71 Blackbird when a missile launched was simply to speed up. No Blackbird was ever shot down.
Greek fire, the Byzantine incendiary weapon that kept burning on water, was so closely guarded that its true recipe is lost forever.
Miguel de Cervantes lost the use of his left hand at the naval battle of Lepanto in 1571, decades before writing Don Quixote.
Henry the Eighth watched his flagship Mary Rose heel over and sink near Portsmouth in 1545. She was raised again in 1982.
Skeletons of Mary Rose archers show bones visibly reshaped by lifetimes of drawing war bows that pulled over 100 pounds.
Sweden's warship Vasa sailed roughly 1,300 meters on her maiden voyage in 1628 before capsizing. Today she fills her own museum in Stockholm.
At Copenhagen in 1801, Nelson raised his telescope to his blind eye and announced he could not see the signal ordering him to withdraw.
Hannibal's double envelopment at Cannae in 216 BC wiped out a huge Roman army and is still taught at military academies.
Thermopylae was never just 300 Spartans. Several thousand other Greeks stood and fought in the pass alongside them.
King Harold of England crushed a Viking army at Stamford Bridge, then force-marched south and lost at Hastings just 19 days later.
After Waterloo, scavengers pulled teeth from tens of thousands of dead soldiers. Dentures across Europe were sold as Waterloo teeth.
The Charge of the Light Brigade happened because a vague order and a misread gesture sent 600 cavalrymen down the wrong valley into Russian guns.
The American Civil War killed more United States soldiers than World War One and World War Two combined.
Wilmer McLean moved away after the war's first big battle touched his farm, only for the Civil War's surrender to be signed in his new parlor.
Stonewall Jackson, the Confederacy's most feared general, was shot in the dark by his own sentries at Chancellorsville.
Mutiny aboard the battleship Potemkin in 1905 began over maggot-riddled meat that the ship's officers insisted was perfectly fine to eat.
American codebreakers confirmed Midway as Japan's target by broadcasting a fake report that the island's water plant had broken down.
United States cryptanalysts broke Japan's top diplomatic cipher, Purple, by building a copy of a machine they had never actually seen.
Admiral Yamamoto, architect of Pearl Harbor, was shot down by American P-38s after codebreakers learned his exact flight schedule.
Shipwrecked in the Pacific, John F Kennedy scratched a rescue message into a coconut shell. That coconut later sat on his Oval Office desk.
Polish officer Witold Pilecki volunteered to be captured and imprisoned in Auschwitz, spending over two years smuggling out intelligence reports.
Sergeant Stubby, a stray dog smuggled to France, warned troops of gas attacks and once caught a German infiltrator by the seat of his trousers.
Army medic Desmond Doss refused to carry a rifle, then lowered around 75 wounded men down a cliff under fire on Okinawa. He got the Medal of Honor.
One-eyed Canadian soldier Leo Major captured the Dutch city of Zwolle almost single-handedly in 1945, mostly by making an enormous racket.
Modena still displays a wooden bucket seized from Bologna during a bloody medieval conflict remembered as the War of the Oaken Bucket.
In 1859 the United States and Britain nearly went to war over a shot pig on San Juan Island. That pig was the standoff's only casualty.
History's last dogfights between piston-engine fighters came in 1969, when El Salvador and Honduras fought days after World Cup qualifiers.
Gassed Russian soldiers at Osowiec fortress counterattacked coughing blood in 1915, an assault remembered as the attack of the dead men.
Dambuster crews smashed German dams with a spinning bomb designed to skip across the water like a stone, hopping over torpedo nets.
More people died building Hitler's V-2 rockets in slave labor factories than were killed by the rockets landing on cities.
USS Nautilus, the first nuclear submarine, crossed beneath the North Pole under the Arctic ice cap in 1958.
One American Ohio class submarine can carry more destructive power than every bomb dropped in all of World War Two.
Serbian air defenses shot down an American F-117 stealth jet in 1999. Pieces of the wreck are displayed in a Belgrade museum.
Norwegian commandos skied into a frozen gorge in 1943 and blew up the heavy water plant feeding Hitler's atomic research, without losing a man.
At Salamis in 480 BC, a smaller Greek fleet lured the Persian navy into narrow straits and shattered it, saving Athens.
Ottoman crews dragged warships overland on greased timbers to bypass Constantinople's harbor chain during the siege of 1453.
Korean admiral Yi Sun-sin never lost a naval battle, once turning back a Japanese fleet of over 130 ships with only 13 of his own.
Royal Navy sailors drew a daily rum ration until 1970. The final issue is still remembered as Black Tot Day.
British sailors picked up the nickname limeys from the citrus juice the Royal Navy issued to hold off scurvy.
USS Ward fired America's first shots of the Pacific war, sinking a midget submarine an hour before Japanese planes reached Pearl Harbor.
Japan floated thousands of bomb-carrying paper balloons across the Pacific. One caused the only World War Two combat deaths on the US mainland.
Ford's Willow Run plant eventually rolled a complete B-24 bomber off the line roughly every 63 minutes.
Surrounded at Bastogne and invited to surrender, American general Anthony McAuliffe sent back a one-word written reply. Nuts.
Finnish troops coined the name Molotov cocktail to mock the Soviet minister who claimed his bombers were dropping bread baskets.
Germany's Maus, the heaviest tank ever built, weighed around 188 tons, far too heavy for almost any bridge to carry.
The German Paris Gun of 1918 shelled Paris from 120 kilometers away, its shells arcing about 40 kilometers above the Earth.
Pheidippides' real run was not 26 miles. Herodotus describes him covering about 240 kilometers to Sparta to beg for help before Marathon.
Kidnapped by pirates as a young man, Julius Caesar joked that he would crucify them once released. After his ransom was paid, he did.
Rome lost three entire legions in the Teutoburg Forest ambush of 9 AD, and Augustus reportedly cried, give me back my legions.
The Hundred Years War between England and France actually lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453.
